clean_text: Allah. Alhamdulillah. In the second of our salah this evening, we listen to a portion of surah Al this is the fifth chapter in the Quran and this was one of and some scholars say the last chapter of the Quran to be revealed to the prophet sallall.aii wasallam. It is certainly the last chapter revealed to him in its subject matter and that is the finishing touches on the rulings of Islam and the intricacies of how to worship Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. This is an incredibly important point because the stories that are mentioned in this chapter are all there to drive that central theme. Of course, Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala mentions that central theme early on in the chapter. He says, "Today I have completed for you all your religion." So this verse was revealed in surah to the prophet sallall.aii wasallam during he stood on the mountain of he gave his very known and very famous last farewell hutba and speech there. And one of the verses that he recited because that verse had just been revealed to him was this verse here in today I have completed and perfected for you your religion your religion and also the word dean doesn't just mean religion but it also means actions and deeds And Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala here says I have fulfilled and completed for you your religion. I have completed for you your deeds how you are to conduct yourselves and I have completed for you the scales of judgment because all of these three issues are dealt with in detail in surah mida. This chapter is incredibly heavy. What do I mean by heavy? It is heavy in its subject matter. It is very grave. In Muslim Im Ahmed, it was narrated that when this chapter of the Quran was revealed to the prophet sallall.aii, he was riding on his camel alas. And when Gabriel descended and started revealing to him this chapter, the weight of the chapter, a physical weight was so heavy and strenuous on the camel that it nearly broke the camel's legs. And so the prophet sallallah.aii alaihi wasallam got off of the camel in order to receive the remainder of the chapter because it began to buckle under the weight of this chapter. One of the stories that are mentioned in this chapter is the story of Habil and in English Cain and Abel. Habil and Kabil are two sons of Adam and a lot of things are mentioned things that are taken from the people of the book and things that are taken from previous scripture and so forth which fill in some of the details. However, when we look just at the text in the Quran and whatever was authentically narrated from the prophet sallall.aii alaihi wasallam. We come to a couple of conclusions about and number one they were both direct sons to Adam. Number two, they were both Muslim. They were both believers and yet they were very different in their attitudes of worship. So Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala says to the prophetam he says subhanahua to the prophetam and convey to them tell them the story the true story of the two sons of Adam and the incident that occurred between them when they both attempted a sacrifice, an offering to Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. This is an important point. They both attempted an offering to Allah. Meaning both of them put forth for themselves a in an act of worship some offering. We don't know from the Quran what this offering was. It's not really relevant. In some narrations it's mentioned not narrations of the prophet sallall.aii alaihi wasallam. But in some narrations of the companions and it's mentioned that was a farmer and Habil was a herder. He had sheep. He was a shepherd. And so Kabil put forth from the yield of his harvest the lowest thing that he had to offer from his harvest. He collected from his harvest as we know fruits and you know vegetables and whatever it is that a person harvests. There's really nice fruits, there's medium and then there's the lowest the bottom of the barrel as they say. He offered to Allah the lowest thing that he had. Why did he do that? There's a number of reasons, but it boils down to he is more focused on the dunya than he is on the hereafter. That's the bottom line. Why? Because all of this harvest, the very nice fruits, the medium fruits, the lowest fruits, they represent dollar signs. They cost different things. And so he took what he couldn't sell and he gave that to Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala because he didn't want to miss out on whatever the dunya had to offer him with regards to the best of what he had and what his crops yielded. Habin was very different. He was a stark opposite. Habib took the best of his herd and put that forward as an offering and a sacrifice to Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. Now notice two Muslims worshiping Allah with the same act of worship yet very different intentions and very different acts. Here is the question. This is one of the things that plagues us as Muslims. A misunderstanding. What is the misunderstanding? The misunderstanding is well, you know, as long as I hit the bare minimum threshold, then I'll be okay. What does that mean? for making salah, as long as I make five prayers and I barely make enough for it to be acceptable by Allah, technically acceptable to where you couldn't say I didn't pray. I did pray, but the prayer was haphazard and it was the bare bare bare minimum. Then Jenna is the what is the eventual abode. Maybe I'll have to go to hell for a little bit. Maybe maybe I'll be punished by Allah for a little bit, but in the long run I'll be able to go to Jenna. And I want anything from Jenna just as long as I get into Jenna eventually. If I make it into the lowest lowest lowest levels of paradise, then it's all good. And Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala tells us in the Quran that he'll remove jealousy and envy from our hearts when we enter paradise. That the lowest level of paradise is seven times better than anything on earth. It's good enough for me. Right? This is the idea that drives this attitude and this result. This is what drives him. You can't technically say I didn't do my my do my service. I fulfilled my service. I gave I made an offering to Allah. Sure it was the lowest and sure but I did it. You can't say I didn't do it. Then you have a stark contrast to that. His brother Habian, he takes the best that he has and he makes that is his offering. He's paying very close attention to what? To the details. Because the details matter. Imagine if you were going to buy a car. Do you not care if there's a scratch here or scratch there? Missing bolt here, missing bolt there? There's a flat tire in the back. Is there a spare? Yeah, there's a Don't. Okay, good enough. Would you put your Would you buy that car with your hard-earned money? If you want to go buy something that you're going to ride in every day or a house, is it okay if there's a little bit of water damage here and there? Not to fix it up in the future. I'm speaking about this is what you're buying. This is what you're going to live in. This is what you're going to drive in. Little some termites underneath the the the floorboards. It's not that bad. It's not that big of an infestation, right? It's good enough. Is there a roof? Technically, it has a roof, right? Technically, it has walls. Technically, it's a house. You can't say it's not a house. Is that how we go about in our lives? Especially with things that matter to us when we go to get married. Is this really how we look at the person that's our potential spouse? She's, you know, missing an eye or she has very terrible stench odor, right? But she's a woman. You can't say she's not a woman, right? He's a man, you know, he's broke. He has no education. You can't say he's not a man. This is not how we live our lives because these things matter to us. We know that we're going to end up living there. If you understand that about the hereafter, you understand that everything you're doing right now, me, myself, this moment, everything that I've done in the in my past, my whole life, and everything else that will come out of my mouth, every action I make, everything I believe, everything I do and I don't do, that is all me preparing my grave. That's all that is. Every time I speak, I'm making an adjustment to my grave. I'm either I'm either turning up the temperature in my grave or I am planting another garden. Those are those are the two things I'm doing. Every time I speak, every time I do something, every time I stand to pray, I am either turning up the temperature, making my grave smaller, making it more inhospitable, making it smell more foul, or I am doing the opposite. I am making my grave just a bit bigger. I am making it just a bit more soothing. I am making it just a bit more comfortable. If you know this, this is what our prophet taught us sallall.aii wasallam. Not only if you know this, but if you believe it with every fiber of your being, you allow yourself to believe it. You don't second guess it. You honestly believe it. Just you are speaking, as real as this world is, that is where I'm going to lay. If we have that, then our actions will be very different. We wouldn't just come to pray. I mean, imagine today if every time you come to pray or stand to pray, you are paid based on you're paid money based on your performance in that prayer. How you dress, how focused you are, how close to the sunnah it was. Is your prayer the prophet's prayer? How are the pillars? How are the sunnah? How are the wedge you bet? And every mistake you make or everything that you are unaware of because you didn't take the time to study it and learn it, you lose money. How would our prayers be? Would they change? Would they be better? Would they get better over time? Would we perfect them? This is really what it is. It's no different than that. The only thing is our reward is just a little bit delayed. Subhanah! Alhamdulillah.

summary:
This lecture discusses Surah Al-Ma'idah (the fifth chapter of the Quran), which was revealed as one of the last chapters to Prophet Muhammad and focuses on completing Islamic rulings and the details of worship. The speaker emphasizes the chapter's heavy spiritual weight and explores the story of Cain and Abel (Kabil and Habil), two sons of Adam who both made offerings to Allah but with drastically different intentions and levels of sincerity. Kabil offered the lowest quality of his harvest due to his attachment to worldly wealth, while Habil offered the best of his herd, demonstrating genuine devotion to Allah.

The central theme is a critique of the Muslim tendency to seek the minimum acceptable standard in worship rather than striving for excellence. The speaker uses practical analogies—buying a car with defects, purchasing a damaged house, or choosing a spouse—to illustrate how Muslims would never accept such compromises in worldly matters, yet approach their religious obligations with the bare minimum effort. He emphasizes that every action and word prepares one's grave and determines one's afterlife, urging believers to perfect their worship through attention to detail, proper learning of Islamic practices, and sincere devotion similar to Habil's approach.
suggested_title: Surah Al-Ma'idah and the Story of Cain and Abel: Excellence in Worship vs. Bare Minimum
catchy_title: Why Muslims Accept Less in Worship
